note:
Richmond，VA。 - 五年前，亚马逊被迫在弗吉尼亚东部的仓库的休息室墙上发布“通知员工”。
 简单地打印通知，只需两种颜色，并用文字填写。但对于任何困扰仔细观察的工人，这是一个显着的声明。亚马逊列出了22种形式的行为，它说它会否认，每个都以大写字母开头：“我们不会。”
 根据纽约时报的通知的照片，亚马逊写道，“如果您是工会支持者，我们不会威胁您的工作。 “我们不会讨论您的联盟或”在您参加联盟活动时“聘用您的监控”。 “我们不会威胁着你的未经指定的报复”因为你是一个工会支持者。我们不会威胁“获得”联盟支持者。
 亚马逊在国际机械师和航空航天工人协会后发布了该清单，在两年漫长的推动中，在切斯特的仓库中的30岁时，在南方的仓库里，距离里士满南部的工会提供了这一点。虽然亚马逊并未承认违反劳动法，但该公司承诺与联邦监管机构的解决方案，告诉工人，它将严格遵守未来规则。
 员工通知和未遂联盟努力尚未报告，突然相关，因为亚马逊面对在美国劳动力骚乱增加。超过二十年，随着互联网零售商从虚拟书店击败了1.5万亿美元的庞然大物，它有力地 - 抵制员工组织的努力。近年来的一些工人在史丹岛，芝加哥，萨克拉门托和明尼苏达州的变化激动，但影响忽略不计。
   去年冠状病毒的到来改变了这一点。它将亚马逊转变为千万困境的基本资源，并重新定义了公司与其仓库工作者的关系。像许多服务业员工一样，他们易受病毒受到影响。随着社会锁定的，如果他们与工作有问题，它们也能够简单地继续前进。
 现在，亚马逊面临着一家仓库的工会投票，艾拉省的仓库。 - 最大，最可行的美国劳动力挑战。近6000名工人直到3月29日，决定是否加入零售，批发和百货商店联盟。劳动力胜利可以激发其他美国社区的工人，亚马逊拥有超过800多名雇用超过50万人的仓库。 
“这是在最艰难的状态下发生的，最艰难的公司，在最艰难的时刻，”罗格斯大学劳工学教授Janice Fine说。 “如果联盟可以在鉴于这三个事实上占上足，它将发送一条消息，亚马逊到处都是。”
 即使工会不盛行，“工会的历史也总是关于失败，”她说。 “努力工作，工人失去，工人再次尝试。”
 当时的努力与监管机构和机械师联盟的文件重建，以及与仓库和联盟官员的前设施技术人员的访谈，提供了最充分的照片鼓励亚马逊工人打开门之一一个联盟 - 公司用来猛击的技术是什么技术，钉了它。
 员工通知是工人的空洞胜利。国家劳工关系委员会，联邦机构与亚马逊谈判的联邦机构，没有权力征收货币处罚。其执法补救措施很少，弱势，这意味着它能够在违反法律中限制反作用及雇主的有限。结算没有公布，所以甚至没有任何公共关系的利益。
 亚马逊是真正的赢家。切斯特的联盟没有进一步尝试。
 亚马逊用于切斯顿的策略在其他地方浮出水面。零售业联盟表示，亚马逊正试图在贝塞姆人中调查员工，甚至改变了交通信号，以防止组织者在离开网站时接近仓库工作者。上个月，纽约司法部将军在诉讼中表示，亚马逊对试图抗议其大流行安全措施不充分的雇员进行了报复。
 亚马逊拒绝说是在2014年和2015年在切斯特的联盟驱动期间遵守劳动法。在一份声明中，它表示，它“符合2016年的国家劳工关系法”，它在发出员工通知和“我们今天继续符合符合要求的。“它在不同的陈述中添加了，它不相信阿拉巴马州的联盟推动“代表了我们员工观点的大多数。” 
彻斯特沉降通知指出一名工人：Bill Hough Jr.是一位领导工会驱动的机械师。通知称，亚马逊向Hough先生发出了警告，他正处于被解雇的边缘。亚马逊表示它会撤销警告。
 霍夫先生（发音怒）是在亚马逊叫做膝盖手术的医院，并说他已经用完了他的医疗假期。由于他不能做他的工作，他说他被告知，这是这是线的结束。
 “甚至在他们对我所做的事情之后，霍夫先生说，”甚至没有怜悯，现在就霍夫先生说。 “那是亚马逊。如果你不能给110％，你就完成了。“
  亚马逊成立于速度，效率和努力的概念 - 很多艰苦的工作。在1994年，将他的第一次帮助申请广告，亚马逊的创始人Jeff Bezos表示，他希望工程师在大约三分之一的大多数人认为可能的时间里，他想要完成工作。“
 亚马逊经理公开警告新兵，如果他们喜欢舒适的东西，这将是一个困难的，也许是不可能的，工作。根据媒体账户和劳工组织者，对于客户服务代表，很难跟上。加班是强制性的。主管将电子邮件发送给主题标题，如“当你死了时可以睡觉。”
 1999年，编号为400的代表是由与美国通信工人隶属的基层集团为目标。亚马逊安装了一个全面的防守。
 如果工人越来越不到温顺，经理被告知，这是一个可能有联盟活动的标志。 Tipoffs包括“喘息的谈话”和“小组抱怨在沉默中分手了主管的方法”，以及增加投诉，在浴室里越来越多的侵略性和跳动。 
亚马逊与较大的文化同步。工会被认为是工业过去的遗物。破坏是一种美德。
 “二十年前，如果您询问政府或工人是否应该对公司的任何限制，答案始终是”没有制约因素“，这是1999年亚马逊运动的劳动组织者Marcus Courtney所说的。 “如果公司每年365天推动人们，每天24小时，戴帽子。”
 当DOT-COM泡沫在2000年爆发时，亚马逊失去了一些发光。一段时间，它的存在是有问题的。
 这也造成了活动家的问题。公司重组并关闭了客户服务中心，尽管亚马逊表示，与工会驱动器没有连接。联合食品和商业工人联盟和Prowitt组委会是一个独立的小组，没有进入亚马逊5,000名仓库工人。
 十年后，2011年，亚马逊的劳动历史上是一个低点。早上在Allentown，PA的报纸透露，亚马逊在夏季热浪在当地仓库期间招聘了医护人员和救护车。倒塌的工人用担架和轮椅去除并被带到医院。
 亚马逊安装了空调，但否则被删除了。在2008年的经济衰退巨大后，对其工作没有缺乏需求 - 而且没有联合抗议工作条件。在欧洲，工会更强大，有零星的罢工。在美国，孤立的仓库罢工不仅仅是少数工人。
   Hough先生在里士满的Reynolds铝厂工作了24年。他曾经看到一名工人在钢滚筒出乎意料时失去四个手指。像对他一样深刻印象的事件：永远不要随便接近设备。 
雷诺斯在霍夫先生在他40多岁的时候关闭了植物的巨大经济衰退。在机械家公会缓冲打击，但他需要另一份工作。经过长时间的失业率，他在2013年加入了亚马逊。
 切斯特仓库，几架飞机载体的大小，一年前开了一年，部分亚马逊的Multibillion-Dollar推动到处都是履行中心。 Hough先生在传送带上致力于带来货物。
 起初，他接受了一般好的标记。 “他有一种伟大的态度，并没有参加负面评论或情况，”亚马逊在2014年3月绩效审查中表示。 “他与所有其他技术人员相处。”
 但霍夫先生说他觉得迫使削减角落以保持皮带跑步。亚马逊骄傲本身就会迅速购买客户，当传送带在危险中陷入困境时。他曾经抗议重启腰带，而他仍在努力。
 “戒掉你的婊子，”霍夫先生说他的经理布莱恩弗莱告诉他，两次。
  拒绝评论的Frye先生不再为亚马逊工作。上个月在Twitter上，他回应了一个新闻报道，称亚马逊正在聘请前F.B.I.代理商处理工人活动，假冒和反垄断问题。
 “这并没有震惊我，”他写道。 “他们做了一些狂野的事情。” 
2014年，Hough先生和其他五名技术人员接近了国际机械师和航空航天工人协会。在德尔德勒在米德尔敦的亚马逊仓库中的技术人员已经举行了工会化努力。如果成功，它将是亚马逊的第一个。
 联盟选举将由国家劳工关系委员会进行。第一步是衡量兴趣。切斯特的30名技术人员中的至少18名返回卡表明他们愿意由联盟代表。
 “签署人们并不是太难，”在那里的工会组织者罗素韦德说。 “但一旦雇主所做的那样，他们会把后台燃烧室放在亚马逊上。然后工人开始失去兴趣。亚马逊花了大量的钱来吓跑员工。“
 董事会预定了2015年3月4日的选举。一项简单的大多数投票将建立联盟代表。
 亚马逊带来了员工资源中心团队 - 基本上，其人力资源部门 - 扭转任何势头。仓库的前技术人员拒绝被命名为恐惧报复，表示，团队的代表追随工人，假装友好，但只是寻求在工会驱动器上的立场。
 如果安全是技术人员的最大问题，也有担心薪酬股权 - 机械师表示，他们得到了不同的数量，以便做同样的工作 - 以及他们对他们的命运缺乏控制。 Hough先生的一部分是，一个工会将使管理层不那么任意。
 “一个人，我记得的只是他的名字是鲍勃，”他说。 “他们将鲍勃分页到控制室，我看到的下一件事就是鲍勃走下了台阶。他脱掉了他的工作背心。我说，'鲍勃，你要去哪儿？“他说，”他们终止了我。“我没有问为什么。这就是它的方式。“ 
若干技术人员表示，他们回忆起在会议上被告知，“你投票给一个联盟，你们每个人都会在明天找工作。”另一方面，根据Hough先生和联盟备忘录，最直言不讳的联盟支持者被描述为“对亚马逊和亚马逊的疾病和疾病”。 （亚马逊向劳工委员会提交，亚马逊表示已经调查了这一事件，并“得出结论，这不能证实。”）
 康斯幸存者霍夫先生表示，该参考局冒犯了他。他拒绝参加该经理的另一个会议。他说他在任何情况下都知道她要说的话：联盟正在取消选举，因为它认为它会失败。亚马逊胜利了。
 2015年3月30日，Hough先生收到了他经理Frye先生的书面警告。
 “你的行为已经被同行/领导者所召唤出负面影响，”它说。包括在“不服从”下是拒绝参加亚马逊胜利公告。亚马逊的另一个事件，可能导致终止。
 机械师联盟于2015年7月向劳工委员会提出了劳工委员会的申诉，据称亚马逊的不公平劳动惯例，包括监督，威胁和“通知员工这将是徒劳的投票给联盟代表投票。”霍夫先生花了八个小时的夏天给出了他的见证。虽然劳动活动家和工会一般认为董事会受到威胁的董事会，但联盟官员表示，正式抗议至少展示有人为他们而战的切斯特技术人员。
 2016年初，亚马逊与董事会定居。两页解决的主要推动是亚马逊将发布员工通知的良好行为，同时承认任何内容。
 1997年至2011年的劳工委员会成员Wilma Liebman审议了时代要求的员工通知。 “我的眼睛是什么异常的是亚马逊的承诺是多么广泛的，以及具体情况，”她说。 “虽然公司没有承认有罪，但该列表提供了可能发生的事情。” 
劳工委员会表示，亚马逊被要求在切斯特60天内将在切斯特习惯于员工通知的所有地方的通知。
 从机械师联盟的观点来看，这并不是太多惩罚。
 “这个帖子基本上是手腕上的禁令，因为亚马逊承诺的违规行为，其中包括谎言，胁迫，威胁和恐吓，”联盟的组织主任Vinny Adceo说。
 提交不公平的劳动措施索赔的另一个原因是，联盟希望与潜在的公司重新启动努力。但大多数支持切斯特驱动器的员工戒掉了。
  在亚马逊的几年里，霍夫先生在健康状况下困扰着。他的癌症的辐射治疗促使几次抚摸。他的妻子苏珊也有健康问题。霍夫先生说，他想知道工会斗争有多少钱促成了他们的问题。他补充说，他不知道要相信谁。
 离开亚马逊后，霍夫先生开始在第一次长期驾驶卡车，后来是一个自卸车。它少付了，但他说他和平。
  当亚马逊征服2014年在特拉华州的联盟驱动器中，零售商表示这是“经理和联营公司之间的直接沟通线条”的胜利。 
亚马逊开发的一个地方，直接沟通在其仓库浴室下面它被称为“分期付款”计划。该分期付款是提供的信息表，例如，有关贝佐斯先生，会议和随机警告的时机，例如这一关于未付休息的时间：“如果你走了负面，你的就业状况将被审查终止。”
  随着工会在白塞勒加热时，直接通信自然是关于这一点。 “你的会费在哪里？”亚马逊在一个摊位张力发布，它在社交媒体上分发。另一个宣称：“工会不能。我们可以。”
 亚马逊还设置了一个网站，告诉工人，他们必须跳过晚餐和学校用品来支付他们的联盟会费。
 在12月，亚马逊发现了一个亲盟集团，亚马逊要求县官员在仓库储存灯上增加“最大绿色时报”，以清除停车场更快。这使得工会帆布难以让他们留下工作的潜在选民。亚马逊拒绝发表评论。
  “应该没有恐吓，没有胁迫，没有威胁，没有反合宣传，”他在一个从未提到亚马逊的视频中说过，但提到了阿拉巴马州的工人“决定是否组织联盟。 “你知道，每个工人都应该有一个免费和公平的选择加入工会。法律保证了该选择。“
  Hough先生，在大流行前采访时，他的一部分想忘记在亚马逊发生的事情。为什么要击败失败？他从工会驱动器中扔掉了所有的论文。他从未见过员工通知，因为他正在从中风中恢复过来。
  “你只是一次踩到我，”他说，坐在他家的里士满郊外。 
他建议，亚马逊的客户只是在那里有多悲惨的工作。
 “我保证你，如果他们的孩子必须在那里工作，他们会在购买事物之前三思而后行，”他说。
  “客户不关心工会。他们不关心工人。他们只是想要他们的包裹，“她说。
 好像在提示，他们的儿子，Brody，进来了。他20岁，是一个家电技术人员。他的母亲告诉他，他的床上有一个包裹。它来自亚马逊，一顶钓鱼帽。 Brody表示，它耗资25美元，制造商网站上的一半价格。
 “我从亚马逊订购，我能找到更便宜的东西，”布罗迪说。这增加了大量的帽子，大约25岁。“我从未为亚马逊工作过。 “我不能讨厌他们，”他说。
 霍夫女士看着她的丈夫。 “如果你自己的儿子不在乎，”她问，不是不客气的，“你将如何让美国公众关心？”
 大流行帮助改变了这一点，将亚马逊的安全问题带到了最前沿。 2月16日，纽约委员会举行的亚马逊诉讼，詹姆斯·詹姆斯举行，该公司去年继续追踪和纪律员工根据其生产率。这意味着工人有限的时间来保护自己免受病毒。西装说，亚马逊对那些抱怨的人报复，向所有工人发送“寒冷的信息”。亚马逊否认了这一指控。 
上周，区域加拿大当局还在多伦多附近的亚马逊仓库中订购了数千名工人，以检测自己，有效地关闭设施。 一位政府发言人表示，大约240名工人最近在那里测试了阳性，即使是该地区的感染率下降。 亚马逊表示这是对决定提出的。  阿拉巴马州现在是一个大测试。 Hough先生担心工会支持者将被压碎。  “他们将威胁或思考，”我不会有工作，亚马逊将取代我，“”他本月通过电话说。 “当一家公司能够秘密地对你做事时，很难承受。”  他补充说，“我希望最好。 对他们来说更多的力量。“
note_en:
RICHMOND, Va. — Five years ago, Amazon was compelled to post a “notice to employees” on the break-room walls of a warehouse in east-central Virginia.
 The notice was printed simply, in just two colors, and crammed with words. But for any worker who bothered to look closely, it was a remarkable declaration. Amazon listed 22 forms of behavior it said it would disavow, each beginning in capital letters: “WE WILL NOT.”
 “We will not threaten you with the loss of your job” if you are a union supporter, Amazon wrote, according to a photo of the notice reviewed by The New York Times. “We will not interrogate you” about the union or “engage in surveillance of you” while you participate in union activities. “We will not threaten you with unspecified reprisals” because you are a union supporter. We will not threaten to “get” union supporters.
 Amazon posted the list after the International Association of Machinists and Aerospace Workers accused it of doing those very things during a two-year-long push to unionize 30 facilities technicians at the warehouse in Chester, just south of Richmond. While Amazon did not admit to violations of labor laws, the company promised in a settlement with federal regulators to tell workers that it would rigorously obey the rules in the future.
 The employee notice and failed union effort, which have not previously been reported, are suddenly relevant as Amazon confronts increasing labor unrest in the United States. Over two decades, as the internet retailer mushroomed from a virtual bookstore into a $1.5 trillion behemoth, it forcefully — and successfully — resisted employee efforts to organize. Some workers in recent years agitated for change in  Staten Island, Chicago, Sacramento and Minnesota, but the impact was negligible.
   The arrival of the coronavirus last year changed that. It  turned Amazon into an essential resource for millions stuck at home and redefined the company’s relationship with its warehouse workers. Like many service industry employees, they were  vulnerable to the virus. As society locked down, they were also less able to simply move on if they had issues with the job.
 Now  Amazon faces a union vote at a warehouse in Bessemer, Ala. — the largest and  most viable U.S. labor challenge in its history. Nearly 6,000 workers have until March 29 to decide whether to join the Retail, Wholesale and Department Store Union. A labor victory could energize workers in other U.S. communities, where Amazon has more than  800 warehouses employing more than 500,000 people.
 “This is happening in the toughest state, with the toughest company, at the toughest moment,” said Janice Fine, a professor of labor studies at Rutgers University. “If the union can prevail given those three facts, it will send a message that Amazon is organizable everywhere.”
 Even if the union does not prevail, “the history of unions is always about failing forward,” she said. “Workers trying, workers losing, workers trying again.”
 The effort in Chester, which The Times reconstructed with documents from regulators and the machinists’ union, as well as interviews with former facilities technicians at the warehouse and union officials, offers one of the fullest pictures of what encourages Amazon workers to open the door to a union — and what techniques the company uses to slam the door and nail it shut.
 The employee notice was a hollow victory for workers. The  National Labor Relations Board, the federal agency that negotiated the settlement with Amazon, has no power to impose monetary penalties. Its enforcement remedies are few and weak, which means its ability to restrain anti-union employers from breaking the law is limited. The settlement was not publicized, so there were not even any public relations benefits.
 Amazon was the real winner. There have been no further attempts at a union in Chester.
 The tactics that Amazon used in Chester are surfacing elsewhere. The retail workers union said Amazon was trying to surveil employees in Bessemer and even changed a traffic signal to prevent organizers from approaching warehouse workers as they left the site. Last month, the New York attorney general  said in a lawsuit that Amazon had retaliated against employees who tried to protest its pandemic safety measures as inadequate.
 Amazon declined to say whether it had complied with labor laws during the union drive in Chester in 2014 and 2015. In a statement, it said it was “compliant with the National Labor Relations Act in 2016” when it issued the employee notice, and “we continue to be compliant today.” It added in a different statement that it didn’t believe the union push in Alabama “represents the majority of our employees’ views.”
  The Chester settlement notice mentions one worker by name: Bill Hough Jr., a machinist who led the union drive. The notice said Amazon had issued a warning to Mr. Hough that he was on the verge of being fired. Amazon said it would rescind the warning.
 Mr. Hough (pronounced Huff) was in a hospital having knee surgery when Amazon called and said he had used up his medical leave. Since he couldn’t do his job, he said he was told, this was the end of the line.
 “There was no mercy, even after what they had done to me,” Mr. Hough, now 56, said. “That’s Amazon. If you can’t give 110 percent, you’re done.”
  Amazon was founded on notions of speed, efficiency and hard work — lots of hard work. Placing  his first help wanted ad in 1994, Jeff Bezos, Amazon’s founder, said he wanted engineers who could do their job “in about one-third the time that most competent people think possible.”
 Amazon managers openly warned recruits that if they liked things comfortable, this would be a difficult, perhaps impossible, job. For customer service representatives, it was difficult to keep up, according to media accounts and labor organizers. Overtime was mandatory. Supervisors sent emails with subject headings like “YOU CAN SLEEP WHEN YOU’RE DEAD.”
 In 1999, the reps, who numbered about 400, were targeted by a grass-roots group affiliated with the Communications Workers of America. Amazon mounted an all-out defense.
 If workers became anything less than docile,  managers were told, it was a sign there could be union activity. Tipoffs included “hushed conversations” and “small group huddles breaking up in silence on the approach of the supervisor,” as well as increased complaints, growing aggressiveness and dawdling in the bathroom.
 Amazon was in sync with the larger culture. Unions were considered relics of the industrial past. Disruption was a virtue.
 “Twenty years ago, if you asked whether the government or workers should be able to put any constraints on companies, the answer always was ‘No constraints,’” said Marcus Courtney, a labor organizer on the 1999 Amazon campaign. “If companies wanted to push people 365 days a year, 24 hours a day, hats off to them.”
 When the dot-com bubble burst in 2000, Amazon lost some of its glow. For a time, its very existence was in question.
 This caused problems for the activists as well. The company reorganized and closed the customer service center, though Amazon said there was no connection with the union drive. The United Food and Commercial Workers Union and the Prewitt Organizing Fund, an independent group, made no inroads organizing Amazon’s 5,000 warehouse workers.
 A decade later, in 2011, came a low point in Amazon’s labor history. The Morning Call newspaper in Allentown, Pa.,  revealed that Amazon was hiring paramedics and ambulances during summer heat waves at a local warehouse. Workers who collapsed were removed with stretchers and wheelchairs and taken to hospitals.
 Amazon installed air conditioning but otherwise was undaunted. After the Great Recession in 2008, there was no lack of demand for its jobs — and no united protest about working conditions. In Europe, where unions are stronger, there were sporadic strikes. In the United States, isolated warehouse walkouts drew no more than a handful of workers.
   Mr. Hough worked as an industrial machinist at a Reynolds aluminum mill in Richmond for 24 years. He once saw a worker lose four fingers when  a steel roller fell unexpectedly. Incidents like that made a deep impression on him: Never approach equipment casually.
 Reynolds closed the plant in the Great Recession, when Mr. Hough was in his mid-40s. Being in the machinists guild cushioned the blow, but he needed another job. After a long spell of unemployment, he joined Amazon in 2013.
 The Chester warehouse, the size of several aircraft carriers, had opened a year earlier, part of Amazon’s multibillion-dollar push to put fulfillment centers everywhere. Mr. Hough worked on the conveyor belts bringing in the goods.
 At first, he received generally good marks. “He has a great attitude and does not participate in negative comments or situations,” Amazon said in a March 2014 performance review. “He gets along with all the other technicians.”
 But Mr. Hough said he had felt pressured to cut corners to keep the belts running. Amazon prided itself on getting purchases to customers quickly, and when conveyor belts were down that mission was in jeopardy. He once protested restarting a belt while he was still working on it.
 “Quit your bitching,” Mr. Hough said his manager, Bryon Frye, had told him, twice.
  Mr. Frye, who declined to comment, no longer works for Amazon. On Twitter last month, he responded to a news story that said Amazon was hiring former F.B.I. agents to deal with worker activism, counterfeiting and antitrust issues.
 “This doesn’t shock me,”  he wrote. “They do some wild things.”
  In 2014, Mr. Hough and five other technicians approached the International Association of Machinists and Aerospace Workers. A unionization effort was already taking place with the technicians at an Amazon warehouse in Middletown, Del. If either succeeded, it would be the first for Amazon.
 The elections for a union would be conducted by the National Labor Relations Board. The first step was to measure interest. At least 18 of the 30 technicians in Chester returned cards indicating their willingness to be represented by the union.
 “It was not too difficult to sign people up,” said Russell Wade, a union organizer there. “But once the word leaked out to Amazon, they put the afterburners on, as employers do. Then the workers started losing interest. Amazon spent oodles of money to scare the hell out of employees.”
 The board scheduled an election for March 4, 2015. A simple majority of votes cast would establish union representation.
 Amazon brought in an Employee Resource Center team — basically, its human resources department — to reverse any momentum. A former technician at the warehouse, who declined to be named for fear of retaliation, said the reps on the team followed workers around, pretending to be friendly but only seeking to know their position on the union drive.
 If safety was the biggest issue for the technicians, there were also concerns over pay equity — machinists said they were paid different amounts for doing the same job — and about their lack of control over their fate. Part of Mr. Hough’s pitch was that a union would make management less arbitrary.
 “One guy, all I remember is his name was Bob,” he said. “They paged Bob to the control room, and the next thing I saw was Bob coming down the steps. He had taken off his work vest. I said, ‘Bob, where are you going?’ He said, ‘They terminated me.’ I didn’t ask why. That’s the way it was.”
 Several technicians said they recalled being told at a meeting, “You vote for a union, every one of you will be looking for a job tomorrow.” At another, the most outspoken union supporters were described as “a cancer and a disease to Amazon and the facility,” according to Mr. Hough and a union memo. (In a filing to the labor board, Amazon said it had investigated the incident and “concluded that it could not be substantiated.”)
 Mr. Hough, a cancer survivor, said the reference had offended him. He declined to attend another meeting run by that manager. He said he had known in any case what she was going to say: that the union was canceling the election because it thought it would lose. Amazon had triumphed.
 On March 30, 2015, Mr. Hough received a written warning from Mr. Frye, his manager.
 “Your behavior has been called out by peers/leaders as having a negative impact,” it said. Included under “insubordination” was a refusal to attend the Amazon victory announcement. Another incident, Amazon said, could result in termination.
 The machinists union filed a complaint with the labor board in July 2015 alleging unfair labor practices by Amazon, including surveilling, threatening and “informing employees that it would be futile to vote for union representation.” Mr. Hough spent eight hours that summer giving his testimony. While labor activists and unions generally consider the board to be heavily tilted in favor of employers, union officials said a formal protest would at least show Chester technicians that someone was fighting for them.
 In early 2016, Amazon settled with the board. The main thrust of the two-page settlement was that Amazon would post an employee notice promising good behavior while admitting nothing.
 Wilma Liebman, a member of the labor board from 1997 to 2011, examined the employee notice at the request of The Times. “What is unusual to my eye is how extensive Amazon’s pledges were, and how specific,” she said. “While the company did not have to admit guilt, this list offers a picture of what likely was going on.”
 Amazon was required to post the notice “in all places where notices to employees are customarily posted” in Chester for 60 days, the labor board said.
 From the machinists union’s point of view, it wasn’t much of a punishment.
 “This posting was basically a slap on the wrist for the violations that Amazon committed, which included lies, coercion, threats and intimidation,” said Vinny Addeo, the union’s director of organizing.
 Another reason for filing an unfair labor practices claim was that the union hoped to restart its efforts with a potentially chastened company. But most of the employees who supported the Chester drive quit.
  Mr. Hough was beset by ill health during his years at Amazon. Radiation treatment for his cancer prompted several strokes. His wife, Susan, had health problems, too. Mr. Hough said he wondered how much the unionization struggle contributed to their problems. He added that he didn’t know whom to trust.
 After leaving Amazon, Mr. Hough began driving trucks, at first long haul and later a dump truck. It paid less, but he said he was at peace.
  When Amazon vanquished the 2014 union drive in Delaware, the retailer said it was a victory for “open lines of direct communication between managers and associates.”
 One place Amazon developed that direct communication was in its warehouse bathrooms under what it called its “inSTALLments” program. The inSTALLments were informational sheets that offered, for instance, factoids about Mr. Bezos, the timing of meetings and random warnings, such as this one about unpaid time off: “If you go negative, your employment status will be reviewed for termination.”
  As the union drive heated up in Bessemer, the direct communication naturally was about that. “Where will your dues go?” Amazon asked in one stall posting, which circulated on social media. Another proclaimed: “Unions can’t. We can.”
 Amazon also  set up a website to tell workers that they would have to skip dinner and school supplies to pay their union dues.
 In December, a pro-union group discovered, Amazon asked county officials to increase “maximum green times” on the warehouse stoplight to clear the parking lot faster. This made it difficult for union canvassers to approach potential voters as they left work. Amazon declined to comment.
  “There should be no intimidation, no coercion, no threats, no anti-union propaganda,” he  said in a video that never mentioned Amazon but referred to “workers in Alabama” deciding whether to organize a union. “You know, every worker should have a free and fair choice to join a union. The law guarantees that choice.”
  Mr. Hough, in an interview before the pandemic, said part of him wanted to forget what had happened at Amazon. Why dwell on defeat? He threw away all the papers from the union drive. He never saw the employee notice because he was recovering from a stroke.
  “You’re only going to step on me one time,” he said, sitting in his home in the outskirts of Richmond.
 Amazon’s customers just don’t know how miserable a job there can be, he suggested.
 “I guarantee you, if their child had to work there, they’d think twice before purchasing things,” he said.
  “The customers don’t care about unions. They don’t care about the workers. They just want their packages,” she said.
 As if on cue, their son, Brody, came in. He was 20, an appliance technician. His mother told him there was a package for him on his bed. It was from Amazon, a fishing hat. It cost $25, Brody said, half the price on the manufacturer’s website.
 “I order from Amazon anything I can find that is cheaper,” Brody said. That adds up to a lot of hats, about 25. “I’ve never worked for Amazon. I can’t hate them,” he said.
 Ms. Hough looked at her husband. “If your own son doesn’t care,” she asked, not unkindly, “how are you going to get the American public to care?”
 The pandemic helped change that, bringing safety issues at Amazon to the forefront. In  a Feb. 16 suit against Amazon, the New York attorney general, Letitia James, said the company continued last year to track and discipline employees based on their productivity rates. That meant workers had limited time to protect themselves from the virus. The suit said Amazon retaliated against those who complained, sending a “chilling message” to all its workers. Amazon has denied the allegations.
 Last week,  regional Canadian authorities also ordered thousands of workers at an Amazon warehouse near Toronto to quarantine themselves, effectively closing the facility. Some 240 workers recently tested positive for the virus there, a government spokeswoman said, even as the rate of infection in the area fell. Amazon said it was appealing the decision.
 Alabama is now the big test. Mr. Hough worries the union supporters will be crushed.
 “They will fall to threats or think, ‘I won’t have a job, Amazon will replace me,’” he said by phone this month. “When a company can do things to you in secret, it’s real hard to withstand.”
 Still, he added, “I’m hoping for the best. More power to them.”
title: 亚马逊粉碎了工会

note_en:
Google is  slowly updating its many iOS applications with App Store privacy labels, detailing the type of data it collects from users. The Google and Chrome applications were recently updated with these new labels, and DuckDuckGo is already using them to its advantage.
  In a post on Twitter this afternoon, DuckDuckGo took issue with Google’s privacy labels for Search and Gmail, while also pointing out that the company only added the labels “ after months of stalling.” DuckDuckGo has often billed itself as a privacy-friendly alternative to Google apps, and it’s now using the App Store privacy labels as more evidence:
 After months of stalling, Google finally revealed how much personal data they collect in Chrome and the Google app. No wonder they wanted to hide it. Spying on users has nothing to do with building a great web browser or search engine. We would know (our app is both in one).
 DuckDuckGo Privacy Browser has been the second most downloaded mobile browser in the US (after Chrome) and, as you might expect, doesn’t collect any data that’s linked to you, making it simple to get the privacy you deserve online.
 The image attached to the tweet shows side-by-side comparisons of the DuckDuckGo App Store privacy label, Chrome, and Google apps. The DuckDuckGo privacy label indicates that it does not collect any data linked to the user, but the same can’t be said for the Google and Chrome apps.
 The privacy labels for Google and Chrome show that data such as location, browsing history, financial information, search history, and more are linked to the user for things like analytics, personalization, and third-party advertising. None of this should necessarily be surprising for Google users, but DuckDuck is using it to its advantage.
 The App Store privacy labels went live in the App Store in December. These privacy “nutrition labels” were first introduced at WWDC over the summer, with Apple saying the goal is to better inform consumers of the privacy practices of individual applications. The App Privacy labels are divided into three sections: “data used to track you,” “data linked to you,” and “data not linked to you.”
 What do you think of DuckDuckGo using the App Store privacy labels as a way to differentiate itself from Google? Let us know down in the comments!

  舒适地球和埃特销售竹板和床上用品;竹服饰有限公司，思想，塔什，免费苍蝇服装，都制作竹服装;咬了一把竹牙刷，可以与无塑料牙膏和牙线一起去。

note_en:
Starting today, the company  is taking pre-orders for its 12 pack boxes of sustainably sourced bamboo paper towels, which will retail for $34.99.
 The Seattle-based company was founded by two ex-Uber employees, Ryan Fritsch and Austin Watkins, who went on to take roles at the logistics startup Convoy, before launching Cloud Paper. Their toilet paper (and now paper towel company) is one of several businesses trying to get consumers to make the switch to bamboo-based consumer products.
  Cozy Earth and  Ettitude sell bamboo sheets and bedding;  The Bamboo Clothing Co.,  Thought,  Tasc,  Free Fly Apparel, all make bamboo clothing; and  Bite has a bamboo toothbrush to go with its plastic-free toothpastes and flosses.
 But ( I’m quoting myself here) Cloud Paper may be the only one to get such super wealthy, high profile investors to flush it with wads of cash. Even so, companies like  Grove,  Tushy,  Reel, and the aptly named  Who gives a crap, Inc. are all angling to wipe up a piece of the  $10.4 billion market for toilet paper.
  The company’s founders are on a mission to make the paper industry more sustainable, according to co-founder Ryan Fritsch, and they’re looking to do it one roll at a time.
 While other companies look at bamboo as a replacement for cotton or plastics, the Cloud Paper co-founder said this company is squarely focused on toilet paper and paper towels because those products make up most of the crap that’s most wasteful in the paper industry.
 The company has already ordered 1 million rolls of toilet paper for production and shipped hundreds of thousands of toilet paper, but the rationale for adoption has shifted, the company said.
 “It definitely had its moment when the COVID shutdowns happened,” said Fritsch. “But [consumption] shifted from a TP panic to ‘There’s an easy and convenient, sustainable, option out there.’ It’s less of an all-out craze,” Fritsch said.
 No less august a body than the National Resources Defense Council  has come out swinging against how much waste is sacrificed to the commode.
 For instance, the logging industry in Canada degrades  over a million acres of its climate-critical forest, in part to feed U.S. demand for toilet paper, according to the NRDC. Demand from the U.S. has grown so substantially that, in recent years, Canada has ranked third globally in its rate of intact forest loss—behind only Russia and Brazil—mostly due to logging, the NRDC said.
 Ninety percent of that is clearcutting, which exacerbates climate change. By the most conservative estimates,  “logging in the boreal releases  26 million metric tons  of carbon through driving emissions from the forest’s carbon-rich soils and eroding the forest’s ability to absorb carbon,” the NRDC wrote in 2020 report. “Toilet paper’s impact is even more severe because, since it is so short-lived, it quickly releases its remaining carbon into the atmosphere. That is why,  according to the Environmental Paper Network , toilet paper made from trees has three times the climate impact as toilet paper created using recycled materials.”
 That’s why wiping out forested paper can be a real boon in the climate fight.
 “The lion’s share of usage is number one is toilet paper and number two is paper towels, after that the size of the market really really shrinks. We’re going to be continuing on the paper space,” said Fritsch.
 The company’s next act will be working with businesses like restaurants, hotels, and even stadiums and arenas to make the swithc.
 “We launched the company as a B2B company. We were working with WeWork and restaurants and the market — if you look at where our paper products were being used,” Fritsch said. “So another big focus will be building products for our commercial customers where there’s higher capacity.”

对于银行和其他金融机构，当他们与外国人互动时，风险增加，所以他们收取更多费用。例如，在美国学习的印度学生，与他们的当地同行相比，借氧汇率以超过13％的利率，如果不是更多的信贷，那么不超过那一半的利率。
 飞跃，一个与旧金山和班加罗尔总部的两岁的创业公司正在试图解决这个问题和许多问题。通过评估他们生成的数据 - 在印度本身，以公平利率向学生提供贷款。
  自从我们最后一次写过跳跃以来，启动已经发展以解决学生面临的其他几个问题，解释了飞跃的联合创始人Arnav Kumar，在接受TechCrunch的采访中。
 Kumar表示，今天的飞跃是帮助学生在入学，签证和测试准备方面有指导。 LEAP还开发了一个社交网络，其中超过了已有超过一大百百万学生正在互相交谈，并使用该平台的其他服务在国外大学获得入场。
 大约十年前，当我想加入工程学院时，我伸出了已经在高校学习的几个人，我已经入围了。事实证明，在印度超过一百万学生每年都在开始他们的大学生活时确切的事情。 （如果我可以完成循环，我已经毕业了，在房子里的某个地方毕业并在CSE中获得学士学位。）
 Kumar表示，Leap的社区今天正在复制离线行为。有些学生，可以肯定，伸向LinkedIn或Facebook上的其他人。但只要专注于一个问题，飞跃正在试图成为希望追求高等教育的学生的社区。 （它的页面在谷歌搜索上索引以获得更好的可见性。）
   “印度是全球海外入学的第二大市场，在几年高等学内，教育入学率上涨了800万。这呈现出巨大的机会，又散布了散落的景观。 Leap是通过其端到端的技术平台和社区 - 第一次方法来解决这个巨大的机会，“丛林企业的创始合作伙伴在一份声明中，Amit Anand表示。 
遍布其他联合创始人Vaibhav Singh在接受采访时表示，每年从印度的学生进入超过5,000名学校和大学的学习，以研究成千上万的课程。
 “所以选择频谱真的很宽，你需要专家，他们可以帮助你做出正确的选择。这是你或你的家人将成为最重要的决定，“他说。
 投资者在这个空间发现了一个机会 - 并且正在跳跃。该初创公司周二表示，它在其系列B轮中筹集了1700万美元。新加坡丛林企业领导新融资轮型，以及红杉市首都印度和猫头鹰企业。初创公司迄今已提高2250万美元。
 全球大流行阻碍了许多印度学生在国外旅行。今年，估计超过700,000名学生们将印度追求高等教育。飞跃联合创始人表示，他们正在努力为今年的150,000名这些学生提供服务。
 LEAP表示计划部署新资本以扩大其技术团队并达到更多地理位置。该启动目前有助于学生在包括美国，加拿大，英国和澳大利亚等几个国家的学院加入大学。辛格说，飞跃也希望雇用一些技术和商业人才。
 “2020年是国际教育的艰难的一年，与Covid相关的旅行限制。我们对去年的LEAP队伍的恢复性印象深刻，在那里他们不仅为数百名学生提供了融资解决方案，而且还通过Sep Scholar扩展，为成千上万的印度学生提供咨询，希望寻求在国外学习。这种垂直综合的策略已经重要加强了掠夺的穆哈斯，“SemoIa印度的校长Ashish Agrawal表示，在启动前写得首次检查跳跃。
note_en:
For banks and other financial institutions, there is an increased risk when they engage with foreigners, so they charge more. An Indian student studying in the U.S., for instance, borrows money at an interest rate over 13%, compared to their local peers who can secure the same amount of credit, if not more, at less than half of that interest rate.
 Leap, a two-year-old startup with headquarters in San Francisco and Bangalore, is attempting to solve this problem and many others. The startup grants loans to students at fair interest rate by evaluating the data they generated — alternative and derived — in India itself.
  Since the  last time we wrote about Leap, the startup has evolved to address several other problems students face, explained Arnav Kumar, co-founder of Leap, in an interview with TechCrunch.
 Kumar said Leap today is helping students with guidance on admission, visa, as well as test preparation. Leap has also developed a social network of sorts where over half a million students are talking to one another and use the platform’s other services to get admission in a college abroad.
 About ten years ago, when I was looking to join an engineering college, I reached out to several individuals who were already studying in the colleges I had shortlisted. Turns out, over a million students in India do the exact same thing each year when they are about to begin their college life. (If I may complete the loop, I did graduate and have a bachelor’s degree in CSE somewhere in the house.)
 Kumar said Leap’s community today is replicating the offline-behavior. Some students, to be sure, reach out to others on LinkedIn, or Facebook. But by just focusing on one problem, Leap is attempting to become the community for students who are looking to pursue higher education. (Its pages are indexed on Google search for better visibility.)
   “India is the second-largest market globally for overseas enrolment, and in just a decade higher education enrolments are up by 8 million. This presents a huge opportunity in an otherwise fragmented landscape. Leap is addressing this huge opportunity through its end-to-end tech platform and a community-first approach,” said Amit Anand, Founding Partner of Jungle Ventures, in a statement.
 Vaibhav Singh, the other co-founder of Leap, said in an interview that students from India take admission in over 5,000 schools and universities abroad each year to study tens of thousands of courses.
 “So the choice spectrum is really, really wide and you need experts who can help you make the right choice. This is the most important decision you or your family will make,” he said.
 Investors have spotted an opportunity in this space, too — and are backing Leap. The startup said on Tuesday that it has raised $17 million in its Series B round. The new financing round was led by Singapore-based Jungle Ventures, along with Sequoia Capital India and Owl Ventures. The startup has to-date raised $22.5 million.
 The global pandemic prevented many Indian students from traveling abroad. This year, more than 700,000 students are estimated to leave India to pursue higher education. Leap co-founders said they are working to serve 150,000 of such students this year.
 Leap said it plans to deploy the fresh capital to expand its tech team and reach more geographies. The startup currently helps students join colleges in several countries including the U.S., Canada, UK, and Australia. Singh said Leap is also looking to hire some tech and business talent.
 “2020 was a tough year for international education with Covid related travel restrictions. We are impressed by the resilience of the Leap team during the last year, where not only have they served hundreds of students with their financing solutions but have also expanded with Leap Scholar providing counselling to thousands of Indian students looking to study abroad. This vertically integrated strategy has materially strengthened the moats for Leap,” said Ashish Agrawal, Principal at Sequoia India, which wrote its first check to Leap before the startup had a product.
title: 跳跃提高1700万美元，以帮助印度学生在国外学习
title_en: Leap raises $17 million to help Indian students study abroad

note_en:
Apple has moved on to its fourth round of the current beta cycle with new developer betas of  iOS 14.5, iPadOS 14.5, tvOS 14.5, and watchOS 7.4 now available for testing.
  New builds can be acquired via the  Apple Developer Center for those signed up for the test program, or via an over-the-air update on devices running the beta software. Public betas usually arrive within a few days of the developer versions, via the  Apple Beta Software Program website.
 The fourth round follows after the third, which arrived on  March 2 for iOS and  iPadOS 14.5, March 3 for  tvOS 14.5, and  March 4 for  watchOS 7.4. The second round arrived on  February 16, while the first beta round landed on  February 1.
 The releases of iOS and iPadOS have been unusual in this cycle, as Apple pulled both the first and third developer betas before reissuing them. No reasons have been offered as to why this occurred.
 The third public betas for iOS and iPadOS were made available on  March 3, and the third watchOS public beta was issued on  March 5.
   So far, the betas for iOS 14.5 and iPadOS 14.5 have included a number of new features. This includes a requirement for apps to ask for a user's permission before allowing access to the device's unique advertising ID, as part of an upcoming App Tracking Transparency feature.
 The  Apple Watch gains the ability to assist with  Face ID unlocking of an iPhone if the user's wearing a mask. Support for PlayStation 5 and Xbox Series X game controllers,  Apple Fitness+ AirPlay 2 casting support, over 200 new emoji, and refinements to  Apple Music have also been spotted.
 Apple has also released a new beta of the  HomePod operating system. The HomePod runs tvOS and the beta is for version 14.5. This is the first beta released for the device since Apple  discontinued the larger HomePod.
 AppleInsider, and Apple itself,  strongly recommend users don't install the betas on to "mission-critical" or primary devices, as there is the remote possibility of data loss or other issues. Instead, testers should install betas onto secondary or non-essential devices, and to make sure there are sufficient backups of important data before updating.
 Find any changes in the new betas? Reach out to us on Twitter at  @AppleInsider or  @Andrew_OSU, or send Andrew an email at   [email protected].

Or ask your  HomePod mini for "AppleInsider Daily" instead and you'll hear a fast update direct from our news team. And, if you're interested in Apple-centric home automation, say "Hey, Siri, play HomeKit Insider," and you'll be listening to our newest specialized podcast in moments.

在IOS 14.5引入的所有新功能中，这使得今天的第四个测试人员发布了开发人员，在操作系统的内部代码中也隐藏了一些更改。这次，9To5Mac发现了参考资料，表明Apple正在研究一种提供独立IOS安全更新的新方法。
  苹果在iOS更新时从未灵活。虽然用户可以选择不安装更新，但如果您未安装最新版本的iOS，则将在没有安全修复的情况下离开。虽然Apple仍然更新IOS 12对于旧的iOS 12，但该公司目前支持的设备无可选择使用最新的安全更新运行此操作系统。
 添加到IOS软件更新菜单中的新部分表示Apple将为iPhone和iPad用户提供独立的安全更新。用户能够选择他们是否只想安装安全更新或完整的iOS更新。
 虽然我们尚未有更多关于此更改的详细信息，但MacOS已经提供了类似的更新方法。当您有一个Mac运行旧版本的操作系统时，acops overs，Apple提供了单独的安全更新，以便用户可以获得安全修补程序和错误修复，而无需安装最新的MacOS版本。
  在iOS 14.5中找到的新代码也提到了一旦下载特定更新，例如安全更新，您可能需要在安装其他可用iOS更新之前删除它。很难讲述Apple计划在iOS中实现这一目标的究竟是如何实现的，但是一个可能性是在IOS 15发布后继续为iOS 14提供安全更新，以便用户可以选择不更新到最新的主要版本，但是继续接收重要的安全补丁。
 上周，Apple为iPhone和iPad用户发布了IOS 14.4.1，它只修复了WebKit中发现的安全违规行为。这是一个更新的一个例子，即Apple可以提供，而无需为此发布新版本的iOS。
  回到2020年，9To5Mac还发现了Apple正在为iOS新的互联网恢复方法工作的参考文献。但是，该功能尚未由公司宣布 - 因此不要指望这种新的更新系统很快就会实施。

note_en:
Among all the new features introduced with iOS 14.5, which had its  fourth beta released today to developers, there are also some changes hidden in the operating system’s internal codes. This time,  9to5Mac found references suggesting that Apple is working on a new method for delivering standalone iOS security updates.
  Apple has never been flexible when it comes to iOS updates. While users can choose not to install an update, you will be left without security fixes if you don’t install the latest version of iOS available. Although Apple still updates iOS 12 for older iPhones and iPads, devices currently supported by the company don’t have the option to run this operating system with the latest security updates.
 A new section added to the iOS software update menu indicates that Apple will provide standalone security updates for iPhone and iPad users. Users would be able to choose whether they want to install only security updates or full iOS updates.
 Although we don’t yet have more details about this change, macOS already offers a similar method of updates. When you have a Mac running an older version of the operating system, such as macOS Mojave, Apple delivers separate security updates so that users can get security patches and bug fixes without having to install the latest macOS version available.
  The new code found in iOS 14.5 also mentions that once you download a specific update, such as a security update, you may need to delete it before installing another available iOS update. It’s hard to tell how exactly Apple plans to implement this in iOS, but one possibility is to continue offering security updates for iOS 14 after the release of iOS 15, so that users can choose to not update to the latest major version, but keep receiving important security patches.
 Last week, Apple released  iOS 14.4.1 for iPhone and iPad users, which only fixed a security breach found in WebKit. This is an example of an update that Apple could deliver without the need to release a new version of iOS just for that.
  Back in 2020,  9to5Mac also found references that Apple was working on a new  Internet Recovery method for iOS. However, this feature is yet to be announced by the company — so don’t expect this new update system to be implemented any time soon.
  You’re reading 9to5Mac — experts who break news about Apple and its surrounding ecosystem, day after day. Be sure to check out  our homepage for all the latest news, and follow 9to5Mac on  Twitter,  Facebook, and  LinkedIn to stay in the loop. Don’t know where to start? Check out our  exclusive stories,  reviews,  how-tos, and  subscribe to our YouTube channel
title: iOS 14.5 Beta代码建议Apple正在处理一种提供独立IOS安全更新的方法
title_en: iOS 14.5 beta code suggests Apple is working on a method for delivering standalone iOS security updates

香港（路透社） - 蚂蚁集团的全球投资者的一些基于2020年的绩效价值超过2000亿美元的中国金融公司估值，人们掌握了这个问题，在其首次公开募股和强迫重组后提供了更清醒的估计。
  这一数字大约是蚂蚁在2018年最后一次筹款后的估值之后的第三名，当时它作为世界上最有价值的未列出的技术公司，远低于3150亿美元，它吹捧了世界上最大的IPO。
 当监管审查在蚂蚁11月份上市提前370亿美元的首次公开发行（IPO）日期，投资者希望坠毁巨大的意外收获。监管机构强制重组作为财务而非技术公司，因为前者通常持续估值，来源和分析师表示，他们的分析更为保守。
 投资者表示，截至10月至12月季度的截至12月季度的业务对监管审查影响。尽管如此，Alibaba Group Holding Ltd联盟的修订估值和上市时间表必须等到它最终确定改造计划。
 投资者对Ant估值的修订估计，这将是第一次确定其退货的估值。
 Warburg Pincus LLC在2020年的盈利和同类公司分析上，蚂蚁在年底大约220亿美元的价值蚂蚁，这两个人表示。其他消息人士称，去年年初，美国私募股权公司以1900亿美元的估值为7000亿美元的股权出售其一部分。
  另一个投资者表示，基于蚂蚁最新的金融数据，其估计与Warburg的估计没有什么不同。 
由于保密限制，人们拒绝确定。 Warburg和Ant拒绝发表评论。
  蚂蚁正在将其公司结构改变为金融控股公司，以便在监管压力后将其视为与银行类似的规则。
 杭州的金融科技巨头于2018年被视为技术公司，当时它在全球最大的单一筹款中估价约为1500亿美元。
  投资者包括私募股权公司Warburg，Carlyle Group Inc，General Atlantic And Silver Lake Partners LP，加上新加坡主权财富基金GIC PTE Ltd和现有股东Bodu Capital和Primavera Capital Group。
 在IPO定价，蚂蚁估值飙升至约3150亿美元，或预测的31倍以上2021次净利润。
 随着改革的收益影响未知，一位投资者表示，它的价值蚂蚁与2018年筹款相同。另一位表示，它标志着其成本的蚂蚁投资，这意味着它没有看到暂时的回报。
  目前尚不清楚，如果是全球投资者如何能够将其投资货币。那些加入2018年筹款的人在对Ant的高度有利的情况下，很少有退出选项，所以独立的人讨论了这个问题。 
据Ant的IPO招股说法，国际投资者在2018年筹款的103亿美元筹款中投资于海上股份，并持有不再携带投票权的所谓C股。招股说明书表明，没有人在蚂蚁委员会上批准。
 投资子公司而不是公司本身对于这么大的投资是不寻常的。中国规则意味着蚂蚁必须在国内融入，更容易获得支付臂支付支付的许可证，限制了其直接离岸筹集资金的能力。
 全球投资者有权获得15％的内部回报率，该公司的年度增长率预计将产生救赎计划，以代替IPO，展示了路透社看到的蚂蚁筹款文件。
 然而，由于C股在暂停首次公开募股中，Ant不太可能提供国际投资者的任何形式的救赎，所以三个人表示。
 蚂蚁在其筹款文件中也没有国家进行任何赎回计划的日期，表明投资者可能必须持有不确定时期的赌注，如果上市不会发生。人们说，他们的任何私人股票销售也需要蚂蚁同意。

HONG KONG (Reuters) - Some of Ant Group’s global investors have valued the Chinese fintech firm at over $200 billion based on its 2020 performance, said people with knowledge of the matter, offering a more sober estimate after the shelving of its IPO and forced restructuring.
  The number is about a third above Ant’s valuation after its last fundraising in 2018 when it emerged as the world’s most-valuable unlisted tech firm, yet is far below the $315 billion it touted for what was set to be the world’s largest IPO.
 Investor hopes for a huge windfall crashed when regulatory scrutiny scuppered the $37 billion initial public offering (IPO) days ahead of Ant’s November listing. Regulator-mandated restructuring as a financial rather than tech firm has since made some more conservative with their analysis as the former typically carry lower valuations, sources and analysts said.
 Ant’s business as of the October-December quarter was little affected by regulatory scrutiny, an investor said. Still, the Alibaba Group Holding Ltd affiliate’s revised valuation and listing timeline must wait until it finalises a revamp plan.
 Investors’ revised estimates of Ant’s valuation, which will determine their returns, are reported here for the first time.
 Warburg Pincus LLC valued Ant at about $220 billion at year-end based on 2020 earnings and comparable company analysis, said two of the people. Early last year, the U.S. private equity firm sold part of its stake for $90 million at a $190 billion valuation in a private trade, other sources said.
  Another investor said its estimate, based on Ant’s latest financial figures, was not very different from that of Warburg.
 The people declined to be identified due to confidentiality constraints. Warburg and Ant declined to comment.
  Ant is changing its corporate structure to that of a financial holding firm following regulatory pressure to subject it to rules similar to those for banks.
 The Hangzhou-based financial technology giant was regarded as a tech firm in 2018 when it raised $14 billion at a valuation of about $150 billion in the world’s largest single fundraising.
  Investors included private equity firms Warburg, Carlyle Group Inc, General Atlantic and Silver Lake Partners LP, plus Singapore sovereign wealth fund GIC Pte Ltd and existing shareholders Boyu Capital and Primavera Capital Group.
 At its IPO pricing, Ant’s valuation soared to about $315 billion, or over 31 times its forecast 2021 net profit.
 With the earnings impact of the revamp unknown, one investor said it valued Ant at about the same as the 2018 fundraising. Another said it marked its Ant investment at cost, meaning it does not see any return for the time being.
  It is unclear if and how global investors in particular will be able to monetise their investment. Those who joined the 2018 fundraising have few exit options under pacts highly favourable to Ant, said separate people with knowledge of the matter.
  International investors in the $10.3 billion U.S. dollar tranche of the 2018 fundraising invested in offshore unit Ant International and hold so-called Class C shares that do not carry voting rights, according to Ant’s IPO prospectus. None were granted a seat on Ant’s board, the prospectus showed.
 Investing in a subsidiary instead of the company itself is unusual for such large investments. Chinese rules meant Ant had to be domestically incorporated to more easily obtain a licence for payment arm Alipay, restricting its ability to raise capital directly offshore.
 Global investors are entitled to a 15% internal rate of return, the annual rate of growth an investment is expected to generate should the firm implement a redemption scheme in lieu of an IPO, showed an Ant fundraising document seen by Reuters.
 As the Class C shares played no part in the suspension of the IPO, however, Ant is unlikely to offer international investors any form of redemption, said three of the people.
 Ant also did not state in its fundraising document a date by which it would conduct any redemption scheme, indicating investors might have to hold their stakes for an indefinite period should the listing not happen. Any private share sale by them would also need Ant’s consent, the people said.
title: 消息来源：一些蚂蚁集团投资者在中国的首次公开募股停止后，公司的价值高于+ 200亿美元，远低于其预期的315亿美元的IPO估值
title_en: Sources: some Ant Group investors have valued the company at $200B+ after its IPO was halted by China, far below its expected $315B IPO valuation

法国行业部长Agnes Pannier-Runacer说，巴黎（Reuters） -  Astazeneca的首席执行官延迟延迟延迟，以延迟延迟其Covid-19疫苗射击其Covid-19疫苗的疫苗，并提供更多他的生产计划的详细信息，法国的行业帕尼尼尼尔·普罗赫·普罗赫说。
  Astazeneca上周表示，到3月底，将在3月底之前向欧盟提供3000万桶，从9000万份合同义务下降，上个月举行的承诺，以提供4000万剂。
 新目标并不保证，因为它涉及荷兰疫苗工厂的监管批准。
 询问首席执行官Pacal Soriot是否受到欧盟的充分压力，Pannier-Runacher告诉法国信息收音机：“我认为他在热门座位，他绝对意识到了。”
  “在任何业务中，都有信托责任，您必须是负责任的。当你不履行合同时，这可能会导致问题，个人问题。“
 Pannier-Runacher表示法国将在3月和4月期待其预期的约1500万剂中的25％，而Astrazeneca没有提供有关生产计划的足够细节。
   Pannier-Runacher的评论作为一些欧盟国家，包括法国和德国，在报告少数受者后暂停使用Astrazeneca疫苗，在某些情况下，在某些情况下致命。 
“一定没有公开的不信任。 如果您看到在其他国家/地区的决定，风险就是对疫苗产生的不信任，“部长说。  世界卫生组织（世卫组织）说没有经过验证的联系，并呼吁人们不恐慌。 预计调查事件的欧洲医疗机构预计将于周四公开讨论该问题。
note_en:
PARIS (Reuters) - AstraZeneca’s chief executive is in the “hot seat” over delays to deliveries of its COVID-19 vaccine shot to European countries and must provide more details of his production plans, France’s Industry Minister Agnes Pannier-Runacher said.
  AstraZeneca said last week it would try to deliver 30 million doses to the European Union by the end of March, down from a contractual obligation of 90 million and a previous pledge made last month to deliver 40 million doses.
 The new target is not guaranteed as it hinges on regulatory approval of a vaccine factory in the Netherlands.
 Asked if chief executive Pacal Soriot was under sufficient pressure from the EU, Pannier-Runacher told France Info radio: “I think he is in the hot seat and that he absolutely aware of it.”
  “In any business, there is a fiduciary responsibility, you have to be accountable. When you do not honour a contract, this can cause problems, individual problems.”
 Pannier-Runacher said France would receive just 25% of some 15 million doses it was expecting in March and April and that AstraZeneca was not giving enough details on production plans.
   Pannier-Runacher’s comments come as a number of EU countries, including France and Germany, have suspended the use of the AstraZeneca vaccine after reports a small number of recipients have suffered blood clots, in some cases fatal.
 “There must not be public mistrust. If you see decisions taken in other countries, the risk is that a mistrust of the vaccine develops,” the minister said.
 The World Health Organization (WHO) said there was no proven link and called on people not to panic. The European Medical Agency, which is investigating the incidents, is expected to address the issue publicly on Thursday.
title: 法国说，Astrazeneca BOSS在疫苗延误中，在“热门座位”中。
title_en: AstraZeneca boss is in the “hot seat” over vaccine delays, France says

在幕后，有两个合作社 - CélineLazthortes和Jonathan Benhamou。 Nicolas Hellinger和Matthieu Pozza是两个剩余的联合创始人，分别担任CTO和CPO。 Lazthores以前共同创立的Leetchi，法国领先的钱盆公司。她还开始了Mangopay，这是一个市场支付解决方案，作为旋转公司。 CréditMutuelArkéa获得了两家公司。
 Benhamou共同创立了Peopledoc，基于云的人力资源服务。 2018年，他的公司被终极软件收购。收购后，他担任公开引用公司的执行官。不久之后，私募股权公司Hellman＆amp; Friedman Capital Partners获得了终极软件。
  去年，他们都花了很多时间在一个名为蛋蛋白的非营利组织上工作。随着140人，他们筹集了740万欧元（880万美元），以购买个人防护设备，并将其送到有需要的医院。这是一个筹款和物流挑战。
 在花很多时间与医疗保健专业人士交谈后，他们决定“至少将接下来的十年献给那些挽救生命的人”，“Laztorthes说。
 这似乎是一个雄心勃勃的赌注，他们意识到这一点。 “我们对医疗保健的任何事情都不知道，就像我们对人力资源和金融都不了解。我们正在进入高度监管的市场，“Benhamou告诉我。
 这就是为什么他们选择专注于特别是癌症护理的一个地区。虽然研究机构在过去几年中取得了一些巨大的进展，但对治疗癌症变得越来越复杂。例如，Benhamou表示，他希望在未来三年内看到300个新的治疗方法。治疗从广泛的光谱治疗缓慢地发展到靶向治疗。
 癌症治疗设施面临三个问题。首先，“人类大脑无法吸收所有这些数据，”Benhamou说。其次，随着预期寿命的增加，每年都有更多的癌症病例。肿瘤委员会将在特定情况下花一分钟半或两分钟，以进行治疗决定。 
第三，由于前两个问题，患者自己留下了。例如，它们患有副作用，因为它们的治疗中没有剂量调整。
  从那里开始，恢复力希望成为医疗团队和患者的癌症治疗的全堆叠软件解决方案。谈到从业者时，弹性将是一个可以增强治疗决策的软件和服务解决方案。公司将分类科学文献，使用机器学习与过去案例和表面临床试验的一些相似之处，基于各种标准。
 涉及患者时，将有一个网络和移动应用程序来访问其癌症的内容和信息。特别是，弹性可以帮助您了解副作用并对待它们。
 “我们的目标是证明该应用程序可以提高患者的生活质量，”Lazthortes说。恢复力也希望利用其应用提出问题并收集数据以改善治疗。
 启动已经将数据科学团队汇总在一起。它将使用自然语言处理来解析科学文学。它还可以与医疗团队合作，仔细检查一切。
 谈到患者之间的相似性时，该公司正在与各医院签署伙伴关系，以获取过去案件的数据。
 由单数筹集了600万美元的资金（500万欧元），该公司由前Alven合作伙伴Raffi Kamber和JérémyUzan成立。 Tech Business Angels Nathalie Balla（La Redoute），Xavier Niel（免费），Jean-Charles Samuelian（Alan），Roxanne Varza（Station F）等也参与。 
今天还有一些医疗保健投资者在今天的资金上，例如CharlesFerté（Astrazeneca），Philippe Dabi（杀杀生物洛杉矶）和托马斯Clozel（Owkin）。  弹性是一个任务驱动的公司 - 公司正在与科学委员会和患者董事会合作。 古斯塔韦·鲁西是世界领先的癌症研究机构之一，也作为一个集体的共同创始人。  这是很多利益相关者，但是在建造医疗保健公司时是正确的事情。 恢复力现在拥有正确的支票和余额系统，以迭代其产品，并推出具有实际改善癌症治疗的机会的产品。
note_en:
Behind the scenes, there are two co-CEOs — Céline Lazorthes and Jonathan Benhamou. Nicolas Helleringer and Matthieu Pozza are the two remaining co-founders acting as CTO and CPO respectively. Lazorthes previously co-founded  Leetchi, the leading money pot company in France. She also started  MangoPay, a marketplace payment solution, as a spinout company. Crédit Mutuel Arkéa acquired both companies.
 Benhamou co-founded PeopleDoc, a cloud-based HR service. In 2018, his company was acquired by Ultimate Software. Following the acquisition, he served as an executive in the publicly quoted company. Shortly after, private equity firm Hellman & Friedman Capital Partners acquired Ultimate Software.
  Last year, they both spent a lot of time working together on a nonprofit called  ProtegeTonSoignant. Along with 140 people, they raised €7.4 million ($8.8 million) in donations to buy personal protective equipment and deliver it to hospitals in need. It was a fundraising and logistics challenge.
 After spending a lot of time talking with healthcare professionals, they decided to “dedicate at least the next ten years to those who save lives,” Lazorthes said.
 It seems like an ambitious bet, and they’re aware of that. “We don’t know anything about healthcare just like we didn’t know anything about HR and finance. We’re entering a market that is highly regulated,” Benhamou told me.
 That’s why they chose to focus on one area in particular — cancer care. While research institutes have made some tremendous progress over the past few years, it has become increasingly more complicated to treat cancer. For instance, Benhamou says he expects to see 300 new treatments over the next three years. Treatment is slowly evolving from broad spectrum treatments to targeted treatments.
 Cancer treatment facilities face three issues. First, “a human brain can’t assimilate all this data,” Benhamou said. Second, as life expectancy increases, there are more cancer cases every year. A tumor board is going to spend a minute and a half or two minutes on a specific case to make a therapeutic decision.
 Third, as a result of the first two problems, patients are left on their own. For instance, they suffer from side effects because there’s no dosage adjustment in their treatment.
  Starting from there, Resilience wants to become a full-stack software solution for cancer treatment for both the medical team and patients. When it comes to practitioners, Resilience will be a software-as-a-service solution that can augment therapeutic decisions. The company will categorize scientific literature, use machine learning to find some similarities with past cases and surface clinical trials based on various criteria.
 When it comes to patients, there will be a web and mobile app to access content and information about their cancer. In particular, Resilience could help you understand side effects and treat them.
 “Our goal is to prove that the app can improve the quality of life of the patients,” Lazorthes said. Resilience also wants to leverage its app to ask questions and collect data to improve treatments.
 The startup is already putting together a data science team. It will use natural language processing to parse scientific literature. It will also work with a medical team to double-check everything.
 When it comes to finding similarities between patients, the company is signing partnerships with various hospitals to get data from past cases.
 Resilience has raised a $6 million funding round (€5 million) led by  Singular, the VC firm founded by former Alven partners Raffi Kamber and Jérémy Uzan. Tech business angels Nathalie Balla (La Redoute), Xavier Niel (Free), Jean-Charles Samuelian (Alan), Roxanne Varza (Station F) and more are also participating.
 There are also some healthcare investors in today’s funding round, such as Charles Ferté (AstraZeneca), Philippe Dabi (Bioclinic) and Thomas Clozel (Owkin).
 Resilience is a mission-driven company — the company is partnering with a scientific board and a patient board. Gustave Roussy, one of the leading cancer research institutes in the world, is also acting as a co-founder in Resilience.
 That’s a lot of stakeholders, but it’s the right thing to do when you’re building a healthcare company. Resilience now has the right system of checks and balance to iterate on its product and roll out a product that has a chance of actually improving cancer treatment.
title: 弹性是一种雄心勃勃的赌注，可以改善癌症治疗方法
title_en: Resilience is an ambitious bet to improve cancer treatment

Cyware，一个纽约创业公司为组织创建了一个平台，用于构建和操作虚拟“网络融合中心” - 人们分享威胁情报，运行端到端安全自动化，并协调并执行360度威胁响应 - 已经挑选了3000万美元的资金，这是它将用于继续发展业务的B系列。
 资金正在通过Advent International和Ten Eventures进行联合领导。去年在Cyber​​seecurity行业中发出了一些浪潮，当时它与交叉点以19亿美元获得ForeScout。同时，十一十一是专门从事网络安全启动的VC。 Prelude基金（Mercato Partners的风险实践），翡翠开发经理，伟大的道路控股和云安全公司Zscaler  - 一系列金融和战略投资者 - 也参加了。在此之前，启动已经筹集了约1300万美元，并未披露其估值。
  去年在业务世界的故事一直是关于在线的一切：人和他们的公司一直在努力工作;消费者正在浏览，通过互联网和应用程序购买和娱乐自己。数字是所有流量的地方。
 在网络安全世界中也出现了不熟悉的是：威胁景观已经发展，因此网络安全的反应已经增长。 Cyware表示，在去年，它在年度经常性收入中看到120％同比增长 - 尽管它没有披露实际的收入数据。其客户是大型企业的混合，也是与他人合作的人来管理网络安全，例如信息共享社区（ISAC），以及代表其他其他人管理网络安全的组织，如托管安全服务提供商和计算机应急响应团队。
 虽然许多人可能在他们的头上独自坐在一个黑暗的房间里，但在他/她的眼中独自坐在黑暗的房间里，但现实更有可能成为一些人之间的合作，提供提示，技术，技术，开发的线程等等。 Cyware，它专注于为合作和创建运营中心提供平台，似乎采用了它的构建相同的方法，一个使合作更容易和部分解决方案的平台。
 它通过安全编程，自动化和响应（称为SOAR），使用团队合作更好并进行更明智的威胁评分，并更好地响应威胁警报。实际上，对于许多安全服务的挑战的一个关键部分是他们跨越组织的多个部分，包括它，合规性，信任和安全，确实安全本身。 Cyware的一个目的是为这些全部创建一个平台，以满足和交换可能在一个地方有所帮助的信息。
 “在过去十年中，安全运营团队难以试图通过大量的威胁数据筛选，并缺乏人类的作用，作为其安全编排策略的一部分，”Anuj Goel，Ph.D.，Cofounder和Ceo Cyware，在一个陈述中。 “我们与虚拟网络融合平台的目标是帮助客户团结一致，通过连接他们的环境中的点来有效地响应高优先级威胁，以及我们所经历的势头是我们在该使命执行的证据证明。本系列B融资将帮助我们继续为客户提供过度溢出，扩大我们的团队，改进我们的平台，真正彻底改变安全运营和威胁情报团队在一起工作。“ 
与CTO AKSHAT JAIN合作的GOEL，将他的牙齿切割成一个大型安全团队，作为CITI的全球网络战略负责人。他也是伦敦战略网络空间中心的顾问，并与其他组织合作，并对恶意黑客问题的协同方法和后果合作。
 投资者不只是在寻找公司的增长，而且还有客户名单 - 他们自己也是网络的领导者 - 这是信任Cyware。
 “在我们越来越连通的环境中，各种规模的公司都要求新的和创新的网络安全解决方案，”校长，校长，Advent Internation说，在一份声明中表示。 “Cyware在龙头企业和主要ISAC之间的早期牵引力反映了其独特的能力，使所有关键安全功能汇集在一起​​，以无缝预期，情境化和纠正威胁。我们期待着借鉴我们在本领域的经验，帮助才华横溢的Cyware团队使其虚拟网络融合平台成为全球企业的黄金标准技术。“
note_en:
Cyware, a New York startup that has created a platform for organizations to build and operate virtual “cyber fusion centers” — spaces for people to share threat intelligence, run end-to-end security automation, and orchestrate and execute 360-degree threat responses — has picked up $30 million in funding, a Series B that it will use to continue growing its business.
 The funding is being co-led by Advent International and Ten Eleven Ventures. Advent made some waves in the cybersecurity industry last year when it partnered with Crosspoint to  acquire Forescout for $1.9 billion. Ten Eleven, meanwhile, is a VC that specializes in cybersecurity startups. Prelude Fund (the venture practice at Mercato Partners), Emerald Development Managers, Great Road Holdings and cloud security firm Zscaler — a mix of financial and strategic investors — also participated. Before this, the startup had raised around $13 million, and it is not disclosing its valuation.
  The story of the last year in the world of business has been about how everything has gone online: people and their companies have been working remotely; consumers are browsing, buying and entertaining themselves over the internet and with apps. Digital is where all the traffic is.
 Unsurprisingly that has also played out in the world of cybersecurity: the  threat landscape has grown, and so cybersecurity responses have grown with them. Cyware said that in the last year it saw 120% year-over-year growth in annual recurring revenue — although it doesn’t disclose actual revenue figures. Its customers are a mix of large enterprises, but also those who both collaborate with others to manage cyber security, such as information sharing communities (ISACs), as well as organizations that manage cybersecurity on behalf of a number of others, such as managed security service providers and computer emergency response teams.
 Although many might have a stereotype of a malicious hacker in their heads who sits alone in a darkened room with a determined look in his/her eye, the reality is more likely to be a collaboration between a number of people, providing tips, technology, threads that are developed and so on. Cyware, in its focus on providing a platform for collaboration and creating operations centers, seems to take the same approach in what it has built, a platform to make collaborating easier and part of the solution.
 It does so through security orchestration, automation and response (known as SOAR), used by teams to collaborate better and make more informed threat scoring, and to respond better to threat alerts. Indeed, a key part of the challenge for a lot of security services is that they cross multiple parts of organizations, including IT, compliance, trust and safety, and indeed security itself. One aim of Cyware is to create a platform for these all to meet and exchange information that could be helpful to others in one place.
 “Over the past decade, security operations teams have had difficulty with trying to sift through copious amounts of threat data and lacked the humans’ role as part of their security orchestration strategies,” said Anuj Goel, Ph.D., cofounder and CEO of Cyware, in a statement. “Our goal with our Virtual Cyber Fusion platform is to help our customers unite their security teams to efficiently respond to high-priority threats by connecting the dots in their environments, and the momentum we’re experiencing is proof that we are executing on that mission. This Series B financing will help us continue to overdeliver for customers, expand our team, improve our platform and truly revolutionize how security operations and threat intelligence teams work together.”
 Goel, who cofounded the company with CTO Akshat Jain, cut his teeth in a big security team, as head of global cyber strategy for Citi. He is also an advisor for the Centre for Strategic Cyberspace in London and has worked with other organizations on collaborative approaches to the problem and consequences of malicious hacking.
 Investors will have not just been looking at the company’s growth, but also the list of customers — themselves also leaders in cyber — that are trusting Cyware.
 “In our increasingly connected environment, companies of all sizes are demanding new and innovative cybersecurity solutions,” said Eric Noeth, Principal, Advent International, in a statement. “Cyware’s early traction among leading enterprises and major ISACs reflects its unique ability to bring together all key security functions to seamlessly anticipate, contextualize and remediate threats. We look forward to drawing on our experience in this sector to help the talented Cyware team make its Virtual Cyber Fusion platform the gold standard technology for enterprises around the world.”
title: Cyware Nabs $ 30m，以帮助组织检测和停止高级网络攻击
title_en: Cyware nabs $30M to help organizations detect and stop advanced cyber attacks

每天晚上在晚餐，在迈克尔格林的家中播放了一个熟悉的仪式：他将一张不锈钢啜饮的杯子滑入他的两岁的女儿，朱丽叶，她为粉红色的塑料嚎叫。经常，绿色给了。但他有一种唠叨的感觉。作为一种环境健康倡导者，他曾争取过啜饮的杯子和婴儿瓶的常见的塑料添加剂双酚A（BPA），其模仿激素雌激素，并与严重的健康问题的长期列表联系起来。 Juliette的Sippy杯是由新一代自由BPA的塑料制成的，而是努力运行加利福尼亚州的环境保健中心的绿色曾遇到过一些这些含有的合成雌激素。
 他在2011年10月思考了这些调查结果作为其周年纪念日准备的中心。那天晚上，绿色，一个带有邋金发和苍白的眼睛的轻微男人，坐在舞台上奠定了朱丽叶的啜饮杯。他讲述了他们的夜间立场。 “当她赢得......每次担心浸出那个类似的杯子的化学品的健康影响是什么时候，”他说，在列出与这些化学癌症，糖尿病，肥胖有关的一些问题之前。为了帮助解决谜语，他说，他的组织计划为雌激素般的化学物质测试自由的吸管杯。
 该中心运送了Juliette的塑料杯，还有17个其他从目标，沃尔玛和婴儿R US购买的其他人，到Certichem，德克萨斯州奥斯汀的实验室。超过四分之一 - 包括朱丽叶 - 回到雌激素活动的阳性。这些结果使实验室的调查结果反映了其对无BPA的塑料的更广泛的国家卫生资助研究所。 Certichem及其创始人乔治比尼特人，他也是德克萨斯州大学神经生物学教授最近在NIH期刊环境卫生角度上共同主调了一篇论文。据报道，“几乎所有”的商业上可获得的塑料是测试的浸出的合成雌激素 - 即使它们没有暴露于已知的条件，以解锁潜在的有害化学物质，例如微波的热量，洗碗机的蒸汽或太阳的蒸汽紫外线。根据Bittner的研究，一些无BPA产品实际上释放了比BPA更有效的合成雌激素。
 雌激素在从骨骼生长到排卵中的一切中发挥着关键作用。太多或太少，特别是在子宫或幼儿期间，可以改变脑和器官的发展，导致生命后的疾病。升高的雌激素水平通常会增加女性乳腺癌的风险。
 在许多常见产品中发现的雌激素化学物质与人类和动物的问题有关。根据一项研究，农药尿道可以转动雄性青蛙女性。 DES曾经规定以防止流产，引起肥胖，罕见的阴道肿瘤，不孕症和在子宫中暴露的人中的睾丸生长。科学家们将BPA与疾病联系在一起，包括哮喘，癌症，不孕症，低精子计数，生殖器畸形，心脏病，肝脏问题和ADHD。 “挑选疾病，从字面上挑选疾病，”密苏里州哥伦比亚大学的生物学教授弗雷德里克·昆卡尔说，这是哥伦比亚省的生物学教授。
  BPA于2008年爆炸入头条新闻，当关于“有毒婴儿瓶”和“毒药”包装的故事变得无处不在。早上好，美国发出了“消费者警报”。纽约时报敦促国会在婴儿产品中禁止BPA。 Sen.Dianne Feinstein（D-Calif）警告在Huffington Post中，“数百万婴儿暴露于危险化学物质隐藏在平面”中。有关父母清除了他们的塑料容器储备，沃尔玛和婴儿的零售商r美国开始从货架上拉瓶和啜饮杯。禁止在婴儿护理物品中禁止BPA的票据在全国各地的各国开始播种。
 今天许多塑料制品，从啜饮杯和搅拌机到Tupperware集装箱，被销售为无BPA。但是Bittner的调查结果 - 其他科学家已经证实了一些 - 这表明许多这些替代方案都分享了使BPA如此可能有害的品质。

塑料和化学利益与Weinberg集团密切合作，曾搭配大烟草的白皮书项目 - 这项努力招募科学家们为二手烟的健康效果创造怀疑。很快威尔伯格将其作为“产品防御”公司，搅拌了白皮书和游说监管机构。它还用自己的科学杂志，监管毒理学和药理学推出了一个贸易群，发表了发现BPA的研究是安全的。
 该行业还与哈佛大学风险分析中心致手，一个与大学公共卫生学院附属的智库有历史，这些坦克具有接受公司捐赠的历史，然后发表对其产品的申请。在20世纪90年代初，其创始人John D. Graham-谁被击中了乔治W.布什的监管Czar- Lobbied，以挤出EPA发现二手烟雾引起肺癌，同时征求菲利普莫里斯的大贡献。
  2001年，随着对BPA堆积的研究，美国化学委员会招募该中心召开一名科学家委员会调查低剂量BPA。据快式公司称，中心支付小组成员12,000美元参加三次会议。他们的最终报告于2004年发布，刚刚少数行业青少年的研究，并得出结论，低剂量BPA暴露伤害人类健康的证据是“非常弱”。到目前为止，大约100种关于低剂量BPA的研究是循环。不是一个单一的行业资助的研究发现它有害，但政府资助科学家的90％的人发现了巨大的效果，从增加乳腺癌对多动症的风险。 12个小组成员中的四个以后坚持认为，由于关于其准确性的问题，该中心将其名称从报告中擦过。
 同时，化学利益，与布什政府进行深深的侵害，让他们隐瞒监管过程。几十年来，食品和药物管理局已经保证了立法者和公众，BPA在低剂量中是安全的。但2008年Milwaukee Journal Sentinel的调查显示，该机构依靠行业游说者追踪和评估BPA研究，并基于其安全评估，主要是两个行业资助的研究 - 其中一项从未发表过的或同行审查。
 该小组委员会向EPA制定其国会授权内分泌破坏筛查的准则，也储备了行业支持的科学家。它包括克里斯·博格特（Chris Borgert）是一个与菲利普莫里斯密切合作的毒理学顾问，以诋毁二手烟的EPA研究。他后来作为国际监管毒理学和药理学学会主席，Weinberg集团赞助的服装，在塑料游说者的办公室遇到了。
 EPA小组成员称Borgert似乎决定了沙袋的过程。 “他总是延迟，总是试图混淆这个问题，”回忆起一个参与者。筛选方法EPA落在行业的剧本中直接来了。除此之外，化学物质将在一系列称为Charles River Sprague Dawley的大鼠上进行测试 - 奇怪的是，奇怪的是，不响应BPA等合成激素。
  如何最好地测试雌激素活动将成为塑料安全斗争的关键前沿。美国化学委员会与不太可能的盟友Peta联合的势力，以对抗动物的大规模化学安全测试。与此同时，Borgert和其他行业资助的科学家们使其他常见方法用于使用雌激素的存在响应的测试 - 不一定告诉我们物质如何影响动物或人类。事实上，巨大的持续的NIH运行研究发现，基于细胞的测试与动物研究紧密履行，这准确地预测了合成雌激素，特别是DES和BPA对人类的影响。 
指导加州大学烟草控制研究和教育中心的Stanton Glantz认为，化学工业的实际目标是具有挑战性的特定测试方法是完全破坏安全测试。 “就像烟草公司一样，他们希望建立一个无法访问的证据，”他说。 “如果他们设置了证据标准，他们赢得了战斗。”
 在BPA战斗的高度期间，VOM Saal定期前往德克萨斯州，并与他的老朋友George Bittner一起蜷缩在餐桌周围，他的房屋俯瞰着奥斯汀郊区的核桃树丛。持有博士学位的Bittner。在斯坦福的神经科学中，是古怪的和令人愤慨的。但是，他对科学有一个聪明的心态，兴趣将它应用于现实世界的问题 - 在他的UT-奥斯汀的实验室中，他已经开发出一种神经 - 再生技术，帮助瘫痪了大鼠在几天内走路。他对VOM Saal对内分泌破坏的研究感兴趣。 “这让我成为我们时代最重要的公共卫生问题，”当我们在他的实验室见面时，Bittner告诉我。 “这些化学品与动物研究中的许多不良反应相关，它们是如此普遍存在。对人类健康摇晃心灵的潜在影响。“
 在20世纪90年代末，Bittner-A Squat，Ruddy男子，含有稀释的红头发和拿破仑炸药玻璃，他们在房地产和商品中进行了整洁的资金 - 开始仔细考虑推出与制造商和公共卫生组织合作的私营公司的想法测试内分泌干扰的产品。他相信这种方法可以帮助提高意识并打破监管逻辑 - 同时也收获利润。
 2002年，Bittner武装了91,000美元的批准，Bittner推出了一副公司：Certichem，测试塑料和其他产品用于合成雌激素和塑料，寻找或开发非感染性替代品。然后，Bittner招募威尔士利用一系列乳腺癌细胞设计特殊测试，该细胞在雌激素存在下迅速繁殖。它具有机器人臂，比处理微观材料的人类更精确。
  但是在长Bittner开始用威尔士州和昆士们的头部开始。 Bittner希望研究人员签署对测试威尔士的权利，而他们坚持认为它属于密苏里大学。最终，他们有一个痛苦的堕落。威尔士州和庇美Saal向NIH提出了申诉，声称Bittner在宣传册中有来自Welshons'实验室的虚假数据。 （Bittner认为他只是从污染的样本中排除了数据;该研究所没有发现错误的证据。抑制乳腺癌蔓延的疗法 - 优化测试方案。到2005年，Bittner在奥斯汀的一家叶茂盛办公室公园开设了商业实验室。他设法吸引了一些大型客户，包括整个食物，其中聘请了Certichem，以在内分泌造成的化学品上建议并测试其一些产品。
 此时，BPA是地球上学习最多的化学品之一。 2006年11月，国立环境保健研究所的昆卡拉和顶级官员召开了一批来自各学科的38名主要研究人员，以评估700加上对该主题的现有研究。该集团后来发布了一份“共识陈述”，阐述了一些寒冷的结论：超过95％的发达国家的人受到癌症和胰岛素的健康问题的“范围内”的BPA水平 - 患早期青春期的抗性。科学家们还发现，对人类类似不利影响的潜力有“关注的巨大原因”，特别是在这些相同障碍中陡峭的上涨。
 与此同时，一个新的研究体系发现BPA以疾病的方式改变了动物的基因。例如，它可以关闭抑制肿瘤生长的基因，从而允许癌症传播。这些遗传变化跨越几代人。 “毒药杀了你，”庇护声解释道。 “像BPA一样的化学物质重新编程你的细胞，并最终导致你的孙子杀死他的疾病。” 
科学家们还揭示了邻苯二甲酸盐和健康问题的内分泌破坏化学品之间的联系，包括人类生殖器异常和不孕症。这些化学添加剂常见于柔软，可柔韧的塑料中，例如在奶嘴和婴儿奶瓶乳头中使用的那些。 2008年，国会通过了一项法律，禁止儿童产品中的六种类型的邻苯二甲酸盐。随着对BPA主流的担忧，国会还向制服科学和监管的努力发动了调查，以及许多国家提出了BPA禁令的国家。
 2009年，BPA联合贸易协会 - 其中包括美国化学委员会，可口可乐和德尔蒙特，其中包括在宇宙俱乐部聚集在华盛顿特区的一名职业卢旺达州的卢迪蒙特圈。根据会议分钟泄露于密尔沃基日记哨兵，该集团探讨了消息传递策略，“包括使用恐惧策略（例如，”您是否希望能够访问婴儿食品？“）。” “圣杯的发言人”与会者同意，是一个“怀孕的年轻母亲，愿意在全国谈论BPA的福利”。“
 即使作为行业制作的防守谈话点，一些公司也开始提供免费的替代品。但他们往往没有打扰他们的其他潜在有毒化合物或合成激素。他们也没有必要：根据美国法律，化学品AR
